intro:
The Philistines were a seafaring people — likely of Aegean origin, among the “Sea Peoples” of Egyptian records — who settled the southwestern coast of Canaan around 1200 BC and became Israel’s most persistent enemy through the period of the judges and the early monarchy. The Table of Nations links them with Egypt through Casluhim and Caphtor (Genesis 10:14), and their land, Philistia, gave the wider region the later name “Palestine.” They organized into a confederation of five cities — Gaza, Ashdod, Ashkelon, Gath, and Ekron — each under a “lord” (Joshua 13:3), and their mastery of iron-working long gave them a military edge over Israel, who lacked smiths (1 Samuel 13:19-22).

Conflict with the Philistines runs through Israel’s history. They captured the ark of the covenant, only to be struck by plagues until they returned it (1 Samuel 5:1-12). Samson, the Spirit-empowered Nazirite judge, harried them for years and brought down their temple of Dagon at his death (Judges 16:28-30). Their champion Goliath of Gath defied the armies of Israel until the shepherd David felled him with a sling and a stone “in the name of the LORD of hosts” (1 Samuel 17:45-50). It was the Philistines who killed Saul and his sons at Mount Gilboa (1 Samuel 31:1-6).

King David finally broke their dominance, subduing them and securing Israel’s borders (2 Samuel 5:25; 2 Samuel 8:1), though border skirmishes continued under later kings. The prophets repeatedly pronounced judgment on Philistia among the nations surrounding Israel (Jeremiah 47:1-7; Amos 1:6-8; Zephaniah 2:4-7). Yet even they fell within the reach of God’s sovereign mercy — “Did I not bring up… the Philistines from Caphtor?” (Amos 9:7).

By the New Testament era the Philistines had lost their distinct identity, their old territory absorbed into Greco-Roman Palestine. In Scripture they stand as the archetypal hostile neighbour — a recurring threat against which God raised up judges and David, demonstrating that Israel’s deliverance rested not on iron or human strength but on the LORD who saves his people.
